preamble: The phonetic transformation from the diphthong /ie/ to the close front rounded vowel /y/ is a documented historical sound change, particularly observed in the development of Old English. This shift involves the monophthongization of the diphthong, where the two vowel elements merge into a single vowel sound, accompanied by rounding.
phoneticEffects: Monophthongization, rounding, raising (of the second element of the diphthong towards a close vowel before rounding)

examples.note: 'older' (Late West Saxon dialect, especially near labial sounds)
'poverty' (Late West Saxon dialect, especially near labial sounds)
'worse' (Late West Saxon dialect, representative of the "wyrsa-type" words, especially near labial sounds)

sources: Steponavicius, A. (1961). The Development of Old English Diphthongs. Language, 37(4), 529-531.
